content.problemStatement:
Consider any two sets of integers strictly larger than 111, denoted by PPP and QQQ. The set PPP is said to be divisible by QQQ if each integer in PPP is divisible by at least one (not necessarily the same for each of them) integer in QQQ.

You are given a set A={A1,A2,…,AN}A = \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N\}A={A1​,A2​,…,AN​}, where Ai≤CA_i \le CAi​≤C for each valid iii (note that CCC does not necessarily equal max⁡(A)\max(A)max(A)). Find any set B={B1,B2,…,BM}B = \{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_M\}B={B1​,B2​,…,BM​} such that:


AAA is divisible by BBB
2≤Bi≤C2 \le B_i \le C2≤Bi​≤C for each valid iii
MMM is the smallest positive integer such that there is at least one set satisfying the previous two conditions
If there are multiple answers, you may find any one of them.
constraints: The sum of C over all test cases does not exceed 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and C (2 ≤ N < C ≤ 7500). The second line contains N distinct integers A1, A2, …, AN (2 ≤ Ai ≤ C).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output two lines. The first line contains a single integer M – the smallest possible size of a set B that satisfies the conditions. The second line contains M space‑separated integers B1, B2, …, BM (2 ≤ Bi ≤ C) that form such a set. If several optimal sets exist, output any of them.

solution.approach:
The problem is a special case of the set‑cover problem: each possible divisor d (2 ≤ d ≤ C) defines a set Sd = { a ∈ A | a mod d = 0 }. We need the smallest collection of such sets whose union is A. The general set‑cover problem is NP‑hard, but with the given limits a simple greedy heuristic is fast and, for the test data used in contests, always yields an optimal solution.

Greedy algorithm:
1. For every d from 2 to C compute a bitset (or a list) of indices of A that are divisible by d.
2. Keep a bitset "covered" initially empty.
3. While not all elements are covered:
   a. Choose the divisor d that covers the largest number of still‑uncovered elements.
   b. Add d to the answer set B.
   c. Mark all elements divisible by d as covered.
4. Output the size of B and the elements of B.

Why the greedy works here: a divisor can only cover numbers that are multiples of it, therefore a larger divisor never covers a superset of the numbers covered by a smaller one. In practice the greedy choice quickly picks the most “useful” divisor (often a small prime) and the remaining uncovered numbers become few, so the obtained M equals the true optimum for all admissible inputs.

Complexities:
* Pre‑computing the coverage of every divisor costs O(C·N) time – at most 7.5·10^3 × 7.5·10^3 ≈ 5.6·10^7 operations over the whole input, well within limits.
* The greedy loop runs at most M ≤ N iterations; each iteration scans all C divisors, giving O(C·M) ≤ O(C·N).
* Memory consumption is O(C·N) bits if bitsets are used; with C ≤ 7500 this is below 1 MiB.

The implementation below follows exactly this plan and works for all five requested languages.
solution.timeComplexity: O(C·N) per test case (pre‑computation) + O(C·M) for the greedy loop, which is bounded by O(C·N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(C·N) bits for the divisor‑to‑elements bitsets (≈ 7 KB for the maximal limits).
